module: Module 2: Differentiation — Definition and Fundamental Properties

sections.text: AP Calculus AB. Module Two — differentiation: definition and fundamental properties. Last module we built limits. This module we cash them in. By the end you'll have a real toolkit — the rules that let you take the derivative of almost anything that shows up on the AP exam.

Look at your car's speedometer. It says sixty miles per hour right now. But think about that — speed is distance over time, and right now is a single instant. There's no time interval. So how can it possibly know? The answer is: it's taking a limit. Average speed over a tiny window, as that window shrinks toward zero. That's the same limit we just built in Module One. That limit has a name. It's called the derivative.

Three things today. One — we'll build the derivative from average rate of change to instantaneous rate, which is just a limit. Two — we'll learn the rule toolkit: power, sum, product, quotient, and the trig and exponential derivatives you need to memorize. Three — we'll connect differentiability to continuity and dodge the classic AP traps.

Start simple. The average rate of change of f on the interval from a to b is f of b minus f of a, all over b minus a. Geometrically — that's the slope of the secant line, the straight line connecting the two points on the curve. This is the algebraic warm-up. We're going to shrink that interval down.

Now squeeze the interval to zero. The secant line rotates and becomes the tangent line at a single point. Algebraically, the derivative at x equals a is the limit, as h approaches zero, of f of a plus h minus f of a, all over h. There's an equivalent point form — the limit as x approaches a of f of x minus f of a over x minus a. Both show up on the AP exam. And the tangent line at a is y equals f of a plus f prime of a times x minus a.

Heads up — there's a notation zoo. f prime of x, d y d x, d d x of f of x, and y prime all mean the same thing. The one to internalize is d y d x. It came from delta y over delta x — a real ratio — taken to the limit. So it's not literally a fraction of two numbers, but because it came from a ratio, it inherits the algebra of fractions. That's why chain rule and u-substitution work. We'll cash that in starting next module.

Sometimes you don't have a formula — just a table of values. The AP exam loves this. To estimate f prime at two, use the symmetric difference quotient: f of two-point-zero-one minus f of one-point-nine-nine, all over zero-point-zero-two. Important — that's an approximation from data, not the definition. The true derivative is still the limit from the previous slide. The symmetric form averages the forward and backward secants for a tighter numerical estimate. And the units of f prime of a are always units of f divided by units of x.

Here's the big one-way rule. If f is differentiable at a, then f is continuous at a. The other direction does not work. Continuous does not imply differentiable. There are four classic failure modes, and you need to recognize all of them. A corner — absolute value of x at zero. A cusp — x to the two-thirds at zero. A vertical tangent — x to the one-third at zero. And any discontinuity at all. So no corner does not mean differentiable. Watch for cusps, vertical tangents, and discontinuities too.

Power rule. d d x of x to the n equals n times x to the n minus one. The exponent comes down out front, and you drop the exponent by one. Works for any real n — integer, fraction, negative. d d x of x to the fifth is five x to the fourth. d d x of one over x — rewrite as x to the minus one — is minus x to the minus two. And bundle in the constant rule, derivative of a constant is zero, and the constant multiple rule, the constant just rides along.

Two more pieces. Sum and difference rule — derivative of a sum is the sum of the derivatives. Clean. And then a derivative table you have to memorize cold. d d x of sine x is cosine x. d d x of cosine x is negative sine x — that minus sign is the most common careless mistake on the AP exam. d d x of e to the x is e to the x — it's its own derivative. And d d x of natural log of x is one over x, for x greater than zero, since natural log is only defined for positive inputs.

Product rule. The derivative of f times g is f prime times g plus f times g prime. Notice — it's not just f prime times g prime. Both terms matter. Worked example: d d x of x squared times sine x. Let f equal x squared, so f prime is two x. Let g equal sine x, so g prime is cosine x. Plug in: two x times sine x plus x squared times cosine x. Done.
Your turn. Find d d x of x times cosine x using the product rule. Set up your f, your g, your f prime, your g prime. Pause the video. Try it. Press play when you're ready.

Walking through it. Let f equal x, so f prime equals one. Let g equal cosine x, so g prime equals negative sine x — that's the minus sign you have to remember. Now plug into the product rule template: f prime g plus f g prime. That's one times cosine x plus x times negative sine x. Clean it up — cosine x minus x sine x. The negative came from differentiating cosine, not from a sign error.

Quotient rule. The derivative of f over g is f prime times g minus f times g prime, all over g squared. The mnemonic that lines up with the formula is d-high times low, minus high times d-low, all over low squared. Worked example: d d x of sine x over x. f prime g is cosine x times x. Minus f g prime is minus sine x times one. Over x squared. Simplifies to x cosine x minus sine x, all over x squared.

Most common quotient-rule mistake on the AP exam — flipping the order in the numerator. If you write f times g prime first, you flip the sign of the whole thing and your answer is wrong. The derivative of the top has to come first. Memorize it as d-high times low minus high times d-low. That ordering is the entire game.

Round out the trig derivatives. d d x of tangent x is secant squared x. d d x of cotangent x is negative cosecant squared x. d d x of secant x is secant times tangent. d d x of cosecant x is negative cosecant times cotangent. Tight pattern to remember: cosine, cotangent, and cosecant all carry the minus sign. Secant does not. The c functions carry the minus.

Recap. The derivative is the limit of the difference quotient — the secant line becoming the tangent. Two equivalent forms, h-form and point-form. The tangent line at a is y equals f of a plus f prime of a times x minus a. Differentiable implies continuous, one direction only — and the four failure modes are corner, cusp, vertical tangent, and discontinuity. Toolkit: power, constant, sum, product, quotient. Memorize sine, cosine, e to the x, natural log, and the other four trig derivatives.

This video is the overview, not the full module. To actually master derivatives, here's the path. One — read OpenStax Calculus Volume One, chapters three-point-one through three-point-six. Two — work through Khan Academy AP Calculus AB practice on derivatives. Three — do the twenty mixed product and quotient problems in your dashboard. Four — book an advisor check-in if the differentiability rules still feel fuzzy. Next up — Module Three: composite, implicit, and inverse functions. That's the chain rule, and it's the most useful single tool in the whole course.
